title: 87.	Find smallest integer x such that φ(x) = n (inverse totient search).
description: 87.	Find smallest integer x such that φ(x) = n (inverse totient search).

examples.explanation: Start with x=1: φ(1)=1 ≠4. x=2: prime factors {2}, φ(2)=2*(1-1/2)=1 ≠4. x=3: prime factors {3}, φ(3)=3*(1-1/3)=2 ≠4. x=4: prime factors {2}, φ(4)=4*(1-1/2)=2 ≠4. x=5: prime factors {5}, φ(5)=5*(1-1/5)=4 =4. Stop and return 5.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log log n) per candidate
approaches.summary: Iterate through integers x starting from 1, compute φ(x) using prime factorization, and stop when φ(x) equals n. This is straightforward but slow due to repeated totient calculations.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(m * sqrt(m) * log log m) where m is the candidate x, potentially exponential in n

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find smallest x with φ(x)=n using brute force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation that iterates through integers, computes φ(x) via prime factorization, and returns the first x where φ(x)=n.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the inverse totient problem by systematically checking integers starting from 1. The intuition is straightforward: since φ(x) is defined for all positive integers, we can compute φ(x) for each x until we find one that equals n. The algorithm begins with x=1 and increments. For each x, it computes φ(x) using the formula φ(x)=x∏(1-1/p) over distinct prime factors p of x. This requires prime factorization of x, which is done by trial division up to √x. When φ(x)=n, we return x. If no such x exists (which is possible for some n), we return -1 or indicate failure. The time complexity is O(m√m log log m) where m is the candidate x, which can be exponential in n since φ(x) grows roughly linearly with x but n is given. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store temporary variables. This approach is simple but inefficient for large n because φ(x) calculations are expensive and we may need to check many x values. Connection to L1: This level uses the φ(x) computation subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling x=1 correctly (φ(1)=1), inefficient prime factorization, and not considering that some n may have no solution. Real code pattern: def smallest_inverse_totient(n): for x in range(1, max_limit): if phi(x) == n: return x return -1.

examples.trace: Start with x=1: φ(1)=1 ≠4. x=2: prime factors {2}, φ(2)=2*(1-1/2)=1 ≠4. x=3: prime factors {3}, φ(3)=3*(1-1/3)=2 ≠4. x=4: prime factors {2}, φ(4)=4*(1-1/2)=2 ≠4. x=5: prime factors {5}, φ(5)=5*(1-1/5)=4 =4. Stop and return 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute φ(x) using prime factorization
ladders.desc: Function that takes an integer x and returns φ(x) by finding its prime factors and applying the product formula.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine computes Euler's totient function φ(x) for a given integer x. The intuition is based on the multiplicative property of φ: if x has prime factorization x=p₁^k₁ * p₂^k₂ * ... * p_m^k_m, then φ(x)=x∏(1-1/p_i) over distinct primes p_i. The algorithm works by first creating a copy of x (call it result), then iterating through potential prime factors starting from 2 up to √x. For each prime p that divides x, we divide x by p while divisible, and update result -= result//p (which implements result*(1-1/p)). After the loop, if x>1, it means x is a prime, so we apply the same update. This efficiently computes φ(x) in O(√x) time due to trial division. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main brute force loop. Connection to L2: This relies on prime factorization as a core technique. Common mistakes include not handling x=1 (φ(1)=1), not updating result correctly, or inefficient factorization. Real code pattern: def phi(x): result = x; temp = x; p=2; while p*p <= temp: if temp%p==0: while temp%p==0: temp//=p; result -= result//p; p+=1; if temp>1: result -= result//temp; return result.

examples.trace: result=12, temp=12. p=2: 12%2=0, divide temp by 2 repeatedly: temp becomes 6, then 3. Update result: result -= 12//2 = 12-6=6. p=3: 3%3=0, divide temp by 3: temp becomes 1. Update result: result -= 6//3 = 6-2=4. p=4: p*p=16 > temp=1, exit loop. temp=1 ≤1, no final update. Return 4.
result=7, temp=7. p=2: 7%2≠0. p=3: 7%3≠0. p=4: p*p=16 > temp=7, exit loop. temp=7>1, so result -= 7//7 = 7-1=6. Return 6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prime factorization by trial division
ladders.desc: Algorithm to find all distinct prime factors of an integer by testing divisibility from 2 upward.
ladders.explanation: This core technique decomposes an integer into its prime factors. The intuition is that any composite number n has a prime factor ≤ √n, so we only need to check divisors up to √n. The algorithm iterates i from 2 to √n. While n is divisible by i, we record i as a prime factor and divide n by i. After the loop, if n>1, it is a prime factor. This yields distinct prime factors when we record only when i divides n initially. Time complexity is O(√n) in worst case (when n is prime). Space complexity is O(k) where k is number of distinct prime factors, but can be O(1) if we process factors immediately. Connection to L1: This factorization is used within the φ(x) computation. Connection to L3: This builds upon checking for prime divisibility. Common mistakes include not handling n=1 (no factors), inefficient by checking all numbers instead of only primes (though checking all is simpler), or not updating n correctly. Real code pattern: def prime_factors(n): factors = []; i=2; while i*i <= n: if n%i==0: factors.append(i); while n%i==0: n//=i; i+=1; if n>1: factors.append(n); return factors.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check if a number divides another
ladders.desc: Determine if an integer a divides integer b without remainder.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept tests divisibility: a divides b if b % a == 0. The intuition is that division yields an integer result. The algorithm simply computes the remainder when b is divided by a. If remainder is 0, a divides b; otherwise, it does not. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This operation is used repeatedly in trial division for prime factorization. Connection to L4: This relies on the modulo operation. Common mistakes include not handling a=0 (division by zero), or confusing divisibility with factors. Real code pattern: def divides(a, b): return b % a == 0.

examples.trace: Compute 12 % 3 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 12. Return True.
Compute 12 % 5 = 2. Since remainder is 2 (not 0), 5 does not divide 12. Return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute remainder (modulo)
ladders.desc: Find the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation computes a % b, the remainder of a divided by b. The intuition is from division: a = b*q + r, where 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo gives r. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 = 3*2 + 1. The algorithm is typically built into programming languages. Time and space complexity are O(1) for fixed-size integers. Connection to L3: This is used to check divisibility. Connection to L5: This builds upon integer division. Common mistakes include misunderstanding negative numbers (in Python, remainder has same sign as divisor, but we assume non-negative inputs here) or dividing by zero. Real code pattern: def mod(a, b): return a % b.

examples.trace: 10 divided by 3 gives quotient 3, remainder 1 because 10 = 3*3 + 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
8 divided by 4 gives quotient 2, remainder 0 because 8 = 4*2 + 0. So 8 % 4 = 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer division and multiplication
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic operations on integers: divide (//) and multiply (*).
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to divide and multiply integers. Division a // b gives the quotient (integer part), and multiplication a * b gives the product. For example, 7 // 3 = 2, and 7 * 3 = 21. The intuition is from basic arithmetic: division splits a into b equal parts, multiplication combines groups. These operations are fundamental to all higher-level algorithms. Time and space complexity are O(1) for fixed-size integers. Connection to L4: Division is used in modulo computation. Connection to higher levels: These operations are used throughout the brute force approach. Common mistakes include confusing integer division with float division, or not handling division by zero. Real code pattern: def int_div(a, b): return a // b; def multiply(a, b): return a * b.

examples.trace: Integer division: 10 // 3 = 3 because 3*3=9 ≤10. Multiplication: 10 * 3 = 30.
Integer division: 5 // 2 = 2 because 2*2=4 ≤5. Multiplication: 5 * 2 = 10.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Prime Factorization O(sqrt(n) * 2^k)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible x by backtracking over prime factors of n+1, using the formula φ(x) = n to construct x from primes. This exploits the multiplicative property of φ to search more efficiently than brute force.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(sqrt(n) * 2^k) where k is the number of prime factors in n+1

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find smallest x with φ(x)=n using backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of inverse totient search using prime factorization and backtracking
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the smallest integer x such that Euler's totient function φ(x) equals n. The key insight is that φ(x) = n implies x must be composed of primes p where (p-1) divides n. We first factorize n+1 to get candidate primes, then use backtracking to construct all possible x values from these primes, keeping track of the smallest valid x. The algorithm works because φ is multiplicative: φ(p^k) = p^(k-1)*(p-1). Starting with n, we try to subtract φ contributions from candidate primes in descending order to ensure we find the smallest x first. We maintain a current product representing x and a remaining n value. When remaining reaches 0, we have a valid x. The time complexity is O(sqrt(n) * 2^k) where k is the number of prime factors in n+1, since we generate subsets of primes and for each, we try multiple exponents. Space is O(k) for recursion depth and prime storage. Common mistakes include not handling the case where no solution exists (return -1), not considering prime powers properly, and not pruning the search when current product exceeds the best found so far. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the prime generation and backtracking search into a complete solution.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=4, find primes p where (p-1) divides 4 → candidates: p=2 (1 divides 4), p=3 (2 divides 4), p=5 (4 divides 4).
Step 2: Sort descending: [5,3,2].
Step 3: Backtrack starting with product=1, remaining=4.
  Try p=5, exponent=1: φ(5)=4, remaining=4-4=0, product=1*5=5 → valid x=5.
  Since we found solution with product=5, and we search primes in descending order, this is smallest.
Step 4: Return 5.

Step 1: n=10, find primes p where (p-1) divides 10 → candidates: p=2 (1 divides 10), p=3 (2 divides 10), p=6 (5 divides 10 but 6 not prime), p=11 (10 divides 10). Valid: 2,3,11.
Step 2: Sort descending: [11,3,2].
Step 3: Backtrack product=1, remaining=10.
  Try p=11, exponent=1: φ(11)=10, remaining=0, product=11 → valid x=11.
  No need to try other combinations since 11 is smallest possible with largest prime.
Step 4: Return 11.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking search over prime factors
ladders.desc: Recursive function to construct x from candidate primes
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the backtracking search that builds possible x values from candidate primes. Given a list of candidate primes (where p-1 divides the original n), a current product representing the partially built x, and remaining n value (original n minus φ contributions used so far), it recursively tries adding prime powers to the product. For each prime p starting from the largest (to find smallest x first), it tries exponents k from 1 upwards while φ(p^k) = p^(k-1)*(p-1) ≤ remaining. It subtracts φ(p^k) from remaining, multiplies product by p^k, and recurses. When remaining becomes 0, it updates the best solution if current product is smaller. The intuition is that we're decomposing n into sums of φ(p^k) values, which corresponds to building x as product of prime powers. The algorithm prunes when product exceeds current best solution. Time complexity is O(2^k * log(n)) in worst case as we try subsets of primes with various exponents. Space is O(k) for recursion stack. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate primes correctly (each prime can be used only once in factorization), not trying enough exponents, and incorrect base case handling. This feeds into L0 as the core search mechanism.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with primes=[5,3,2], remaining=4, product=1, best=∞.
Step 2: Try p=5, k=1: φ=5^(0)*(4)=4 ≤ remaining=4.
  New remaining=4-4=0, new product=1*5=5.
  remaining=0 → update best=min(∞,5)=5.
  Return (no need to try other primes since remaining=0).
Step 3: Return product=5.

Step 1: primes=[3,2], remaining=2, product=1, best=∞.
Step 2: Try p=3, k=1: φ=3^0*2=2 ≤ remaining=2.
  New remaining=0, new product=3.
  Update best=3.
Step 3: Return product=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating candidate primes from n
ladders.desc: Find all primes p where (p-1) divides n
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: to find x such that φ(x)=n, x must be composed of primes p where (p-1) divides n. This comes from the formula φ(p^k) = p^(k-1)*(p-1). For x to have φ(x)=n, each prime factor p of x must contribute some multiple of (p-1) to n. Therefore, we find all primes p such that (p-1) is a divisor of n. We do this by iterating d from 1 to sqrt(n) where d divides n, then checking if p = d+1 is prime. We collect all such primes. The intuition is that we're reversing the totient calculation: given n, we find what prime factors could have produced it. This reduces the search space dramatically from all numbers to only those built from these candidate primes. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n) * sqrt(p)) for divisibility checking and primality testing. Space is O(k) for storing primes. Common mistakes include forgetting that p must be prime (not just d+1), not handling large n efficiently, and missing divisors. This feeds into L1 by providing the candidate primes for backtracking.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=4, find divisors d of 4: 1,2,4.
Step 2: For each d, check if p=d+1 is prime:
  d=1 → p=2, prime → include 2.
  d=2 → p=3, prime → include 3.
  d=4 → p=5, prime → include 5.
Step 3: Sort descending: [5,3,2].

Step 1: n=10, divisors: 1,2,5,10.
Step 2: Check primes:
  d=1 → p=2, prime → include 2.
  d=2 → p=3, prime → include 3.
  d=5 → p=6, not prime → skip.
  d=10 → p=11, prime → include 11.
Step 3: Sort descending: [11,3,2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing φ(p^k) for prime powers
ladders.desc: Calculate Euler's totient for numbers of form p^k
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept computes φ(p^k) where p is prime and k ≥ 1. The formula is φ(p^k) = p^(k-1) * (p-1). This works because among the numbers 1 to p^k, only multiples of p are not coprime with p^k, and there are p^(k-1) such multiples. Thus, φ(p^k) = p^k - p^(k-1) = p^(k-1)*(p-1). For example, φ(2^3) = 2^2*(2-1) = 4*1 = 4. The intuition is that for prime powers, the totient calculation simplifies dramatically. This is crucial for our backtracking because when we consider adding prime factor p^k to our number x, we need to know how much φ value it contributes. We use this to subtract from remaining n during search. Implementation involves computing p^(k-1) efficiently (using pow or iteration) and multiplying by (p-1). Time complexity is O(log k) for exponentiation. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in exponent (using k instead of k-1), not handling k=0 case (though k≥1 in our use), and integer overflow for large p^k. This feeds into L2 as we need φ(p^k) to check if (p-1) divides n when generating candidates.

examples.trace:
Step 1: p=2, k=3.
Step 2: Compute p^(k-1) = 2^(2) = 4.
Step 3: Multiply by (p-1)=1 → 4*1=4.
Step 4: φ(2^3)=4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Prime checking up to sqrt(n)
ladders.desc: Determine if a number is prime using trial division
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether a given integer p > 1 is prime by testing divisibility by numbers from 2 to sqrt(p). A prime number has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. The algorithm works by iterating i from 2 to floor(sqrt(p)). If p is divisible by any i, it's composite; otherwise, it's prime. We only need to check up to sqrt(p) because if p = a*b with a ≤ b, then a ≤ sqrt(p). The intuition is that any composite number must have a factor no larger than its square root. For example, to check if 17 is prime: sqrt(17) ≈ 4.12, test divisibility by 2,3,4 → none divide 17, so 17 is prime. Time complexity is O(sqrt(p)). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include checking up to p-1 (inefficient), not handling p ≤ 1 correctly (non-prime), and not optimizing with even number check first. This feeds into L3 as we need primality testing when generating candidate primes from divisors of n.

examples.trace:
Step 1: p=7 > 1.
Step 2: sqrt(7) ≈ 2.65, floor=2.
Step 3: Test i=2: 7%2=1 ≠ 0.
Step 4: i=3 > 2.65, stop.
Step 5: No divisors found → 7 is prime.

Step 1: p=9 > 1.
Step 2: sqrt(9)=3.
Step 3: Test i=2: 9%2=1 ≠ 0.
Step 4: Test i=3: 9%3=0 → divisible.
Step 5: Found divisor → 9 is composite.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Divisors of an integer
ladders.desc: Find all positive divisors of a number n
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: finding all positive integers d that divide n evenly (n % d == 0). For a number n, divisors come in pairs: if d divides n, then n/d also divides n. We can find all divisors by iterating i from 1 to sqrt(n). For each i where n % i == 0, we add both i and n/i to our list (being careful not to add duplicate when i == n/i). The intuition is that divisors are symmetric around sqrt(n). For example, divisors of 12: pairs are (1,12), (2,6), (3,4). By checking up to sqrt(12)≈3.46, we find 1,2,3 and their complements 12,6,4. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)). Space is O(d) where d is number of divisors. Common mistakes include not handling n=0 or n<0 properly, forgetting to sort the divisors, and incorrect handling of perfect squares. This feeds into L4 as we need to find divisors of n to generate candidate primes p = d+1.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=6, sqrt(6)≈2.45, floor=2.
Step 2: Iterate i=1 to 2:
  i=1: 6%1=0 → add 1 and 6/1=6.
  i=2: 6%2=0 → add 2 and 6/2=3.
Step 3: Sort: [1,2,3,6].

Step 1: n=9, sqrt(9)=3.
Step 2: Iterate i=1 to 3:
  i=1: 9%1=0 → add 1 and 9/1=9.
  i=2: 9%2=1 ≠0 → skip.
  i=3: 9%3=0 → add 3 and 9/3=3 (but 3 already added from i=1's complement? Wait, careful: when i=3, n/i=3, but we should avoid duplicate since i == n/i). Actually, from i=1 we added 1 and 9; from i=3 we add 3 only once.
Step 3: Sort: [1,3,9].

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Sieve O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Precompute φ(x) for all x up to a bound using a sieve, then scan to find the smallest x with φ(x) = n. This trades space for time by avoiding repeated totient computations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find smallest x with φ(x) = n using DP sieve
ladders.desc: Complete solution: precompute φ(x) for all x up to bound, then scan to find smallest x with φ(x) = n.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the inverse totient problem efficiently by trading space for time. The intuition is that computing φ(x) individually for each candidate x would be too slow (O(n√n)), but we can compute all φ values up to a bound simultaneously using a modified sieve algorithm in O(n log n) time. Once we have the φ array, finding the smallest x with φ(x) = n becomes a simple O(n) scan. The key insight is that Euler's totient function φ(x) can be computed using the formula φ(x) = x ∏(1 - 1/p) for prime factors p of x. The sieve approach initializes φ[i] = i for all i, then for each prime p, multiplies φ[k] by (1 - 1/p) for all multiples k of p. This works because when we process prime p, all numbers divisible by p get their φ value reduced by factor (p-1)/p. After processing all primes up to n, φ[i] contains the correct totient values. We then scan from 1 upward to find the first i where φ[i] = n. The space complexity is O(n) for storing φ array. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling n=1 correctly (answer is 2, not 1 since φ(1)=1 but we want smallest x>1), 2) Setting bound too small (need bound > n since φ(x) ≤ x-1 for x>1), 3) Off-by-one errors in sieve loops. The connection to L1 is that this level uses the sieve from L1 as a black box, then adds the scanning logic.

examples.trace:
1. Set bound = n+1 = 5 (since φ(x) ≤ x-1 for x>1)
2. Initialize φ array: φ[0]=0, φ[1]=1, φ[2]=2, φ[3]=3, φ[4]=4, φ[5]=5
3. Sieve primes up to 5: primes = [2,3,5]
4. Process prime 2: for k=2,4: φ[2] *= (1-1/2) = 2*0.5=1; φ[4] *= 0.5 = 4*0.5=2
5. Process prime 3: for k=3: φ[3] *= (1-1/3) = 3*(2/3)=2
6. Process prime 5: for k=5: φ[5] *= (1-1/5) = 5*0.8=4
7. Final φ array: [0,1,1,2,2,4]
8. Scan x from 1: x=1 φ=1≠4, x=2 φ=1≠4, x=3 φ=2≠4, x=4 φ=2≠4, x=5 φ=4=4 ✓
9. Return 5

1. Set bound = n+1 = 11
2. Initialize φ array size 12: φ[i]=i for i=0..11
3. Sieve primes up to 11: [2,3,5,7,11]
4. Process prime 2: multiples 2,4,6,8,10: φ[2]=1, φ[4]=2, φ[6]=3, φ[8]=4, φ[10]=5
5. Process prime 3: multiples 3,6,9: φ[3]=2, φ[6]=2, φ[9]=6
6. Process prime 5: multiples 5,10: φ[5]=4, φ[10]=4
7. Process prime 7: multiple 7: φ[7]=6
8. Process prime 11: multiple 11: φ[11]=10
9. Scan: x=1 φ=1≠10, x=2 φ=1≠10, ... x=10 φ=4≠10, x=11 φ=10=10 ✓
10. Return 11

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sieve to compute φ(x) for all x up to n
ladders.desc: Compute Euler's totient function for all numbers 1..n using modified sieve.
ladders.explanation: This is the core computational engine for the full solution. The intuition is that instead of computing φ(x) independently for each x (which would require factorization), we can compute all values simultaneously using a sieve similar to the Sieve of Eratosthenes. We initialize φ[i] = i for all i. Then for each prime p from 2 to n, we iterate through multiples of p and multiply φ[k] by (1 - 1/p). This works because φ(x) = x ∏(1 - 1/p) over prime factors p of x. When we process prime p, all numbers divisible by p get their φ value reduced by factor (p-1)/p. After processing all primes, each φ[i] has been multiplied by (1-1/p) for every prime factor p of i, giving the correct totient value. The algorithm: 1) Create array phi of size n+1, initialize phi[i]=i. 2) Create boolean prime array, mark all as prime initially. 3) For p from 2 to n: if prime[p] is true: for multiple k = p to n step p: phi[k] = phi[k] * (p-1) / p; mark multiples of p as non-prime. Time: O(n log log n) for sieve + O(n log n) for φ updates ≈ O(n log n). Space: O(n). Common mistakes: integer division issues (use phi[k] = phi[k] / p * (p-1) to avoid floats), off-by-one in array sizes, not handling φ[1]=1 correctly. Connection to L0: this subroutine provides the φ array that L0 scans through.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Euler's totient formula and multiplicative property
ladders.desc: Understanding φ(x) = x ∏(1 - 1/p) and how it enables sieve computation.
ladders.explanation: This is the mathematical foundation that makes the sieve approach possible. Euler's totient function φ(x) counts numbers 1 ≤ k ≤ x that are coprime to x (gcd(k,x)=1). The key formula is: if x has prime factorization p₁^a₁ · p₂^a₂ · ... · pₖ^aₖ, then φ(x) = x ∏(1 - 1/pᵢ). For example, 12 = 2²·3, so φ(12) = 12·(1-1/2)·(1-1/3) = 12·0.5·(2/3)=4. The intuition: for each prime factor p, we exclude the multiples of p, which are 1/p of the numbers, leaving (1-1/p) fraction. Since primes are coprime, we multiply these fractions. The multiplicative property: if gcd(a,b)=1, then φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b). This explains why the sieve works: when we process prime p, we multiply φ[k] by (1-1/p) for all k divisible by p, which correctly accounts for the p factor in k's prime factorization. The sieve processes primes in increasing order, ensuring each composite number gets multiplied by (1-1/p) for all its prime factors. Time/space: understanding this formula is conceptual, but implementing it gives O(n log n). Common mistakes: forgetting φ(1)=1, misunderstanding that formula requires distinct primes (exponents don't matter: φ(p^a)=p^a - p^{a-1} = p^a(1-1/p)), not handling large n where product might overflow. Connection to L1: this formula is exactly what the sieve implements.

examples.trace:
1. Prime factorization: 12 = 2² × 3
2. Apply formula: φ(12) = 12 × (1 - 1/2) × (1 - 1/3)
3. Compute: 12 × 0.5 × (2/3)
4. Step: 12 × 0.5 = 6
5. Step: 6 × (2/3) = 4
6. Verify: numbers coprime to 12 ≤12: 1,5,7,11 = 4 numbers ✓

ladders.title: Building Block — Sieve of Eratosthenes for prime generation
ladders.desc: Generate all primes up to n using classic sieve algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This is a prerequisite for computing φ values efficiently. The Sieve of Eratosthenes finds all prime numbers up to n by iteratively marking multiples of primes as composite. Intuition: start with all numbers assumed prime, then for each number i from 2 to √n, if i is prime, mark all multiples j = i², i²+i, ... as composite. Why start at i²? Because smaller multiples (i×2, i×3, ..., i×(i-1)) were already marked by smaller primes. The algorithm: 1) Create boolean array is_prime[0..n], set all to true. 2) is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=false. 3) For i from 2 to √n: if is_prime[i]: for j = i*i to n step i: is_prime[j]=false. 4) Collect all i where is_prime[i]=true. Time: O(n log log n) – nearly linear. Space: O(n). Common mistakes: not handling n<2 correctly, starting j from i instead of i² (works but less efficient), iterating i to n instead of √n. Optimization: only process odd numbers after 2. Connection to L2: we need primes to apply Euler's totient formula; the sieve efficiently provides them.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize is_prime[0..10] all true
2. Set is_prime[0]=false, is_prime[1]=false
3. i=2 (prime): mark multiples 4,6,8,10 false
4. i=3 (prime): start at 9, mark 9 false
5. i=4 (not prime, skip)
6. i=5 > √10≈3.16, stop
7. Collect primes: 2,3,5,7

1. Initialize is_prime[0..20] true
2. Mark 0,1 false
3. i=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 false
4. i=3: mark 9,15 false (12,18 already marked)
5. i=4: not prime
6. i=5: mark 25>20, none
7. i=5 > √20≈4.47, stop
8. Collect primes ≤20: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) and coprimality
ladders.desc: Compute GCD and determine if two numbers are coprime.
ladders.explanation: This is fundamental to understanding φ(x), which counts numbers coprime to x. Two numbers a and b are coprime (relatively prime) if gcd(a,b)=1. The Euclidean algorithm computes gcd efficiently: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) until b=0, then gcd=a. Intuition: any divisor of a and b also divides a-b, and by extension a mod b. The algorithm: function gcd(a,b): while b≠0: a,b = b, a%b; return a. For coprime check: return gcd(a,b)==1. Time: O(log min(a,b)) for Euclidean algorithm. Space: O(1). Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly, confusion with LCM (lcm(a,b)=a*b/gcd(a,b)). Example: gcd(12,18): 12,18→18,12→12,6→6,0→gcd=6, so not coprime. gcd(12,25): 12,25→25,12→12,1→1,0→gcd=1, so coprime. Connection to L3: sieve doesn't directly use GCD, but understanding coprimality is essential for grasping what φ(x) counts.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer arrays and basic iteration
ladders.desc: Create, initialize, and iterate through arrays of integers.
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute prerequisite for implementing any sieve algorithm. An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible by index. For our problem, we need integer arrays to store φ values and boolean arrays for prime flags. Basic operations: 1) Declaration: create array of size n+1 (since we use indices 0..n). 2) Initialization: set each element to a value, often in a loop. 3) Access: read or write element at index i. 4) Iteration: loop through indices to process elements. Intuition: arrays allow O(1) access to any element by index, making them ideal for sieve algorithms where we frequently update values at specific indices. Example: to initialize array phi of size 6: phi = [0]*6 gives [0,0,0,0,0,0]; then for i in range(6): phi[i]=i gives [0,1,2,3,4,5]. Time: O(n) to initialize/iterate. Space: O(n) for array storage. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (size n vs n+1), forgetting zero-indexing, modifying array while iterating (usually safe for sieves). Connection to L4: arrays are the data structure used to store GCD results or φ values.

examples.input: Create array of size 5 initialized to indices
examples.trace:
1. Declare array arr of size 5
2. Initialize: for i from 0 to 4:
   i=0: arr[0]=0
   i=1: arr[1]=1
   i=2: arr[2]=2
   i=3: arr[3]=3
   i=4: arr[4]=4
3. Result: [0,1,2,3,4]
